perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, Employee is currently an employee of Heritage and the Bank intends to offer employment to the Employee as an at-will employee to provide services for and on behalf of the Bank immediately upon the Effective Time;
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, Employee is currently an employee of Heritage and the Bank intends to offer employment to the Employee as a permanent employee to provide services for and on behalf of the Bank immediately upon the Effective Time;
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'at-will employee' to 'permanent employee' creates a contradiction because the contract later specifies at-will employment. Indiana law recognizes both types of employment, and this change introduces ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: Indiana Code recognizes that employment can be terminated at will, but replacing 'at-will' with 'permanent' introduces a contradiction later in the document where at-will employment is specifically mentioned. This undermines the at-will agreement, and this creates a conflict with IC 22-5-3-1 concerning employment agreements.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies 'at-will employment,' while the changed text replaces it with 'permanent employee.' The Perturbation Explanation states that this creates a contradiction because the contract later specifies at-will employment. This is a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction because 'permanent' and 'at-will' have different legal meanings, creating ambiguity and potentially making the contract unenforceable. The Law Explanation also highlights this contradiction, stating that replacing 'at-will' with 'permanent' undermines the at-will agreement. Since both snippets are unusable, I am using the remaining evidence to make my assessment.

perturbation.original_text: Employee shall devote his/her full working time and attention to the performance of those services for the Banking Organization which are assigned to him/her from time to time and to perform such services in a faithful, honest and diligent manner and in the best interest of such Banking Organization.
perturbation.changed_text: Employee shall devote reasonable time and attention to the performance of those services for the Banking Organization which are assigned to him/her from time to time and to perform such services with ordinary care.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'full working time and attention' and 'faithful, honest and diligent manner and in the best interest of such Banking Organization' to 'reasonable time and attention' and 'ordinary care' changes the standard of care expected of the employee and introduces legal uncertainty and conflicts with duties of loyalty under Indiana law. Indiana law expects employees to act in the best interest of their employer.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Indiana law regarding duty of loyalty.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens the employee's duty of loyalty and care compared to the original text. This potentially contradicts the implied duty of loyalty an employee owes to their employer under Indiana law and the Restatement (Second) of Agency § 387, which requires an agent to act solely for the benefit of the principal in all matters connected with the agency.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: duty of loyalty | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you duty of loyalty The duty of loyalty is one of the fiduciary duties owed by a company’s directors . The duty of loyalty requires the directors to place the interests of the company and the shareholders before any of their personal interests. The directors’ actions such as diverting corporate assets , opportunities, or information for personal gain can certainly violate their duty of loyalty. Additionally, the duty of loyalty can be also violated when a director usurps a corporate chance by taking advantage of information valuable to the company and instead gains profits from it for personal benefits. In addition, the duty of loyalty places additional responsibilities upon the directors. For instance, the directors are required to keep confidential, but not to disclose or misuse any information that they receive in their capacity as directors. Also, the directors need to report every conflict of interest no matter whether real or perceived, to the company. In the cases where conflicts do exist, the directors should disclose all relevant information. To avoid violations of the duty, the directors are supposed to disclose personal conflicts they may have to the board of directors so that a disinterested vote could be taken without them. Directors are also supposed to be prepared to present any opportunities relevant to the corporation to the board before trying to benefit from them. [Last reviewed in July of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords llcs-corporations-partnerships Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS legal practice/ethics business law business organizations corporations wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the employee's duty of loyalty by changing the requirement from 'full working time and attention' and acting 'in the best interest' to devoting 'reasonable time and attention' and acting with 'ordinary care.' This contradicts the implied duty of loyalty an employee owes to their employer. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines the 'duty of loyalty' as requiring directors (and by extension, employees) 'to place the interests of the company and the shareholders before any of their personal interests.' The modified text, by requiring only 'reasonable time and attention' and 'ordinary care,' potentially allows the employee to prioritize their own interests over the Banking Organization's, thereby contradicting this duty. This constitutes a legal contradiction because the altered standard of care undermines the core principle of acting in the employer's best interest.

perturbation.original_text: b. “Confidential Information” shall mean any and all materials, records, data, documents, lists, writings, and information (whether in writing, printed, electronically stored, computerized, on disk or otherwise, including, but not limited to, all copies, summaries, analyses, drafts, and extracts) relating or referring in any manner to trade secrets (as currently defined under applicable law, including, but not limited to, the Indiana Uniform Trade Secrets Act and any amendments or successor statutes) of a Banking Organization, as well as other non-public financial or proprietary information of a Banking Organization including, but not limited to, business reports, business plans, projections, income statements, profit and loss statements, business strategies and/or strategic plans, internal audits, sales information, sales techniques, business costs, product pricing, budgets, research and development, intellectual property, software and/or computer programs, marketing strategies or ideas, marketing plans or materials, business development plans or strategies, records or information relating to customers or account holders, customer lists, inventions, and processes, systems, methods, documentation or devices which are unique or proprietary to the business of or services or products of a Banking Organization.
perturbation.changed_text: b. “Confidential Information” shall mean only those materials explicitly marked as 'Confidential' by the Banking Organization and directly related to trade secrets as defined by the Indiana Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.explanation: Narrowing the definition of 'Confidential Information' only to explicitly marked materials drastically reduces the scope of protection compared to the original definition, which included various types of non-public information. This weakens the protection afforded to non-public information which is a violation to the original agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Indiana Uniform Trade Secrets Act (IUTSA), Ind. Code § 24-2-3-1
perturbation.law_explanation: The Indiana Uniform Trade Secrets Act protects trade secrets regardless of whether they are explicitly marked as such. The modified definition contradicts the IUTSA by requiring explicit marking, thus undermining the protection of valuable confidential information. This also contradicts the original intent of the agreement.

perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' by requiring explicit marking, which contradicts the Indiana Uniform Trade Secrets Act (IUTSA). The IUTSA protects trade secrets regardless of explicit marking. The changed text undermines the protection of valuable confidential information, which goes against the intent of the original agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 2, citing Indiana Code § 24-2-3-1, indicates that the chapter on trade secrets aims to 'make uniform the law with respect to the subject matter of this chapter among states enacting the provisions of this chapter.' By requiring explicit marking, the modified contract deviates from the uniform application and potentially weakens the protection intended by the IUTSA, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Therefore, in consideration of the covenants of all Financial Institutions contained herein, Employee agrees that during Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution, and for a period of one (1) year immediately following the termination of Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution (“Restriction Period”), for whatever reason and regardless of how Employee’s employment was terminated, Employee shall not, directly or indirectly (including, but not limited to, through other individuals, any partnership, corporation or business entity in which Employee has an ownership interest or serves as an officer, employee, independent contractor, representative, agent or consultant), either for Employee’s own benefit or the benefit of any other Person: a. Solicit, divert, or take away (or attempt to Solicit, divert or take away) any Banking and Financial Services to or from any Customer or Potential Customer; or b. advise, persuade or induce (or attempt to advise, persuade or induce) any Customer or Potential Customer to terminate, reduce, limit, or change the Customer’s services or business relationship, or the Potential Customer’s contemplated services or business relationship, with a Financial Institution; or c. directly provide Banking and Financial Services to, or directly accept Banking and Financial Services from, any Customer or Potential Customer.
perturbation.changed_text: Therefore, in consideration of the covenants of all Financial Institutions contained herein, Employee agrees that during Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution, and for a period of one (1) year immediately following the termination of Employee’s employment with a Financial Institution (“Restriction Period”), only if the employee is terminated with cause, Employee shall not, directly or indirectly (including, but not limited to, through other individuals, any partnership, corporation or business entity in which Employee has an ownership interest or serves as an officer, employee, independent contractor, representative, agent or consultant), either for Employee’s own benefit or the benefit of any other Person: a. Solicit, divert, or take away (or attempt to Solicit, divert or take away) any Banking and Financial Services to or from any Customer or Potential Customer; or b. advise, persuade or induce (or attempt to advise, persuade or induce) any Customer or Potential Customer to terminate, reduce, limit, or change the Customer’s services or business relationship, or the Potential Customer’s contemplated services or business relationship, with a Financial Institution; or c. directly provide Banking and Financial Services to, or directly accept Banking and Financial Services from, any Customer or Potential Customer.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the condition that the non-solicitation agreement only applies if the employee is terminated 'with cause' significantly limits the scope of the restriction. This creates uncertainty about when the non-solicitation clause is enforceable, potentially conflicting with the original intent and enforceability under Indiana law. It contradicts the 'regardless of how Employee's employment was terminated' clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Indiana law on non-compete agreements; *Hahn v. Drees*, 62 N.E.3d 469 (Ind. Ct. App. 2016)
perturbation.law_explanation: Indiana courts examine the reasonableness of non-compete agreements, and this modification injects ambiguity. By restricting the non-solicitation agreement to terminations 'with cause,' the contract potentially contradicts established case law that examines the overall reasonableness of such agreements regardless of the termination reason. *Hahn v. Drees* highlights the court's focus on the balance between protecting the employer's legitimate interests and the employee's ability to earn a living, and this change could upset that balance, potentially rendering the restriction unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a significant condition ('only if the employee is terminated with cause') that directly impacts the enforceability of the non-solicitation agreement. This contradicts the original text's 'regardless of how Employee’s employment was terminated' clause, creating a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation also states that this modification injects ambiguity and potentially contradicts established case law that examines the overall reasonableness of such agreements regardless of the termination reason. The scraped law snippet 1 is not relevant, but the perturbation explanation strengthens the claim that the modification creates uncertainty about the non-solicitation clause, thus impacting its enforceability. This aligns with the Misaligned Terminology definition of a legal contradiction, which arises when contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.
